what_it_does: It is a short methodological preamble that tells readers how to read a breakdown of a client's statements. It defines three interpretive lenses — SPECIFIED (what was literally said), IMPLIED (what is necessitated or assumed but unstated), and ADDITIONS (plausible but unraised fallout) — and clarifies that the breakdown is analytical surface, not a decision or specification.
observations: YAML frontmatter marks this as a noticeboard block with id item-bed5b395, title 'BD0 · How to read the breakdown below', source claude_code, channel dragnet-development, and a part_of link to board://item-11da929d.
The body is purely instructional/meta: it explains a three-lens reading framework.
It explicitly addresses the case of a non-developer client ('Tim').
It states that the IMPLIED layer is usually the larger, more important body for a non-developer client.
It offers ADDITIONS because the client 'explicitly cannot know to ask'.
It ends with a strong disclaimer that none of this is a decision or a spec; it is 'the analyzed surface for diverse downstream thinking.'
Created and updated timestamps are identical; history contains a single 'filed' event.
standouts: The explicit three-lens taxonomy: SPECIFIED / IMPLIED / ADDITIONS.
The clear disclaimer that the content is interpretive analysis, not specification or decision.
The framing that unstated assumptions and unasked opportunities are central to understanding a non-developer client's request.
conventions: Uses YAML frontmatter with id, address, type, source, state, title, author_session, channel, thread, links, created, updated, and history.
Uses board:// URI identifiers for items and links.
Uses a 'BD0' prefix in the title, suggesting an ordered breakdown item.
Markdown body with a top-level heading matching the title.
Declares a part_of relationship to another board item in the links section.
concerns: The ADDITIONS lens could introduce analyst-inferred desires that the client has not actually endorsed.
No criteria are given for how to decide whether something is IMPLIED versus ADDITIONS.
No examples are provided, so downstream readers must infer how to apply the lenses in practice.
notes: A meta-reading guide that defines three interpretive lenses for a client breakdown and insists the result is analysis, not a spec.
questions: What is the breakdown below and which items does it introduce?
Who is Tim and what is his relationship to the project?
What criteria separate IMPLIED from ADDITIONS?
How will downstream work use this analyzed surface?
What is the parent item-11da929d and how does this guide relate to it?
purpose_vs_actual: Matches: the file states it is a guide for reading a breakdown, and that is exactly what it provides.
intention_signals: It is trying to establish a shared, disciplined way to read a client's spoken requirements so that hidden assumptions and unstated opportunities are visible without being mistaken for agreed scope.
novelty: The explicit three-lens taxonomy and the candid justification that ADDITIONS are offered because the client cannot know to ask; also the self-aware framing that the whole artifact is non-normative analysis.
contribution: A reusable reading-guide pattern for client breakdowns and a clear boundary marker distinguishing interpretive analysis from specification or decision.
summary_for_embedding: This noticeboard item is a methodological preamble defining SPECIFIED, IMPLIED, and ADDITIONS lenses for reading a client breakdown, stating the result is interpretive analysis rather than a decision or spec.
intention_for_embedding: It was written to orient readers on how to interpret a three-lens client-requirement breakdown and to bound that interpretation as non-normative.
